1: YHWH commands Joshua to establish the cities of refuge. The command had been previewed three times in the Torah: Exodus 21:13 (the principle), Numbers 35:9-34 (the detailed law), Deuteronomy 19:1-13 (the Mosaic exposition). Joshua 20 is the implementation — the covenant instruction given at Sinai finally applied to the land. The three-stage announcement (law → exposition → application) mirrors how Israel received all major covenant institutions.
2: ṯēn lākem ʾet-ʿārê hammiqlāṭ ʾăšer-dibbartî ʾălêkem bəyaḏ-mōšeh — 'designate the cities of refuge of which I spoke to you by the hand of Moses.' The phrase bəyaḏ-mōšeh (by the hand of Moses) authorizes the command by tracing its pedigree: this is not Joshua's innovation but Moses's mandate finally enacted. ʿārê hammiqlāṭ (H5892 H4733) — 'cities of shelter/asylum'; miqlaṭ (H4733) derives from the root meaning to receive or gather in (qālaṭ).
3: The purpose: lānûs šāmmāh rōṣēaḥ makkēh-nepeš bišgāgāh bəlîdāʿat — 'so that a killer who strikes a person accidentally without knowledge may flee there.' Two qualifiers define the eligible manslayer: bišgāgāh (H7684, by mistake/inadvertently — from šāgag, to err, go astray) and bəlî-dāʿat (without knowledge/intent — H1097 H1847). The legal distinction between accident and intent (šəgāgāh vs. zaḏôn) underlies the entire Torah's graduated penalty system. The city of refuge is explicitly not available to the willful murderer (cf. Numbers 35:16-21). The gōʾēl haddām (H1350 H1818, blood-avenger/kinsman-redeemer of blood) is the kin responsible for avenging unlawful death.
4: wəʿāmaḏ petaḥ šaʿar hāʿîr wəḏibber bəʾoznê ziqnê hāʿîr hahiʾ ʾet-dəḇārāyw wəʾāsəpû ʾōtô — 'he shall stand at the entrance to the gate of the city and state his case to the elders.' The city gate (šaʿar H8179) is the locus of public judicial proceedings in ancient Israel — where contracts, legal matters, and disputes were conducted before witnesses. The manslayer must present his case (dəḇārāyw, his words) to the zəqēnîm (H2205, elders) — the civic authority. The verb ʾāsap (H622, to gather in, receive) is the same root as miqlaṭ's related meaning: the elders 'gather him in' to the city's protection.
5: If the blood-avenger pursues the manslayer, the elders shall not hand him over — because 'he struck his neighbor unintentionally and was not hating him before.' The Hebrew causal chain: bişgāgāh (inadvertently) + wəlōʾ-śōnēʾ hûʾ lô mitmōl šilšôm (he was not an enemy of him previously) — the absence of prior enmity is the evidentiary test for accidental killing vs. murder. The elders function as a court of preliminary adjudication before the full assembly trial.
6: wəyāšaḇ bāʿîr hahîʾ ʿaḏ-ʿomḏô lipnê hāʿēḏāh lammišpāṭ ʿaḏ-môt hakkōhēn haggāḏôl ʾašer yihyeh bayyāmîm hāhēm — 'he shall live in that city until he stands before the assembly for judgment, until the death of the high priest who is serving in those days.' Three temporal markers: (1) until standing before the ʿēḏāh (H5712, assembly) for trial; (2) until the death of the kōhēn haggāḏôl (high priest); (3) after the priest's death, free to return home. The death of the high priest functions as a general amnesty — the legal logic is debated, but the pattern is clear: death of the covenant mediator releases the community from certain legal claims.
7: The three western cities of refuge are designated: Qeḏeš (H6943, sanctuary/holy place) in Galilee in the hill country of Naphtali; Šekem (H7927, shoulder/ridge) in the hill country of Ephraim; Qiryat Arbaʿ (H7153, city of four, = Hebron) in the hill country of Judah. The three cities span the north-south length of western Canaan — geography ensures that no potential manslayer is more than a half-day's journey from a city of refuge.
8: The three eastern cities (Transjordan) are designated: Beṣer (H1221, fortress/stronghold) in the wilderness plateau for Reuben; Rāmôt (H7216, heights) in Gilead for Gad; Gôlān (H1474, circuit/captive) in Bashan for Manasseh. The six cities form a network — three west, three east — ensuring symmetrical access for all twelve tribes. The Transjordan's three cities serve the two-and-a-half tribes who settled east of the Jordan in Numbers 32.
9: ʾēlleh hāyû ʿārê hammuʿāḏāh — 'these were the appointed cities' (môʿāḏ H4150, appointed meeting-place — the same word as in ʾōhel môʿēd, tent of meeting). The six cities of refuge are 'meeting places' of mercy and justice. The closing phrase: wəlōʾ-yāmût bəyaḏ gōʾēl haddām ʿaḏ-ʿomḏô lipnê hāʿēḏāh — 'so that he would not die by the hand of the blood-avenger until he had stood before the assembly.' The institution functions as due process — not exoneration, but the guarantee of a fair hearing before execution. The phrase gēr (H1616, sojourner/foreigner) in the last clause confirms the cities' universal scope: even the non-Israelite resident may access the asylum.
